title: Dep Chain Smoke Test
Step 1: Add SMOKE_A comment
description: Add a single-line comment at the very top of src/types.ts (before any existing comments) that says exactly: // SMOKE_A. Do not change anything else. When done, call the task_complete tool with status 'complete'.

title: Step 2: Add SMOKE_B comment
description: Add a single-line comment at the very top of src/types.ts (before any existing comments, including // SMOKE_A which should already be there) that says exactly: // SMOKE_B. Do not change anything else. When done, call the task_complete tool with status 'complete'.

title: Step 3: Verify both comments exist
description: Read src/types.ts and verify that both // SMOKE_B and // SMOKE_A comments exist at the top of the file (in that order, B before A). If they are both present, call the task_complete tool with status 'complete'. If either is missing, call task_complete with status 'failed' and explain what's missing.

title: Reverse: super-ralph
description:
Clean-room reverse engineering of the super-ralph CLI at src/.
Produces a specification at docs/specs/super-ralph-spec.md that is complete enough
to build from without ever seeing the original source material.

title: Deep study of source material
description:
## Objective
Exhaustively study and understand the source material described below.
Your goal is to build a deep mental model of WHAT the system does and
WHY — not to copy or paraphrase the source. You are preparing to write
a clean-room specification.

## What "Clean-Room Specification" Means
A clean-room spec describes a system's observable behavior, interfaces,
constraints, and design rationale in enough detail that a competent
engineer could reproduce an equivalent system from the spec alone,
without ever seeing the original source material. The spec must:
- Describe BEHAVIOR, not implementation (what it does, not how the code is structured)
- Be complete enough to build from (no "see the source for details")
- Capture the WHY behind design decisions (so the implementer makes good choices)
- Include edge cases, error handling, and failure modes
- Specify interfaces precisely (exact function signatures, data formats, protocols)
- Define constraints (performance, security, compatibility requirements)

## Source Material

### super-ralph CLI
**Type**: Local source code at `src/`
Read ALL files under this path. For each file:
- Understand its role in the system
- Identify the public API (exported functions, classes, types)
- Trace how it connects to other files
- Note any implicit behavior (side effects, state mutations, event emissions)

Also read these supporting files to fully understand the project context:
- `package.json` — dependencies, scripts, bin entry point
- `README.md` — stated purpose and usage
- `AGENTS.md` — agent instructions
- `tsconfig.json` — build configuration
- Any other config files at the project root

The source code consists of 19 TypeScript files:
beads.ts, config.ts, decompose.ts, engine.ts, events.ts, forward.ts,
index.ts, init.ts, interactive.ts, opencode.ts, output-parser.ts,
progress.ts, reverse.ts, run-state.ts, run-status.ts, skills.ts,
template.ts, timeout.ts, types.ts

## Instructions
1. Study ALL source material listed above — be exhaustive
2. Read every file, trace execution flows, understand the full behavior surface
3. Map out the CLI's command structure, options, and workflows
4. Understand the relationship between the "engine" and the various modes (forward, reverse, decompose, interactive)
5. Understand how beads/tasks are tracked and managed
6. Understand how sub-agents (opencode) are spawned and controlled
7. Understand the event system and progress reporting
8. Understand configuration, initialization, and state management
9. Create a structured analysis document at `docs/specs/super-ralph-analysis.md` containing:
   - Summary of what the system does (behavioral, not structural)
   - All identified features and capabilities
   - All interfaces (CLI commands, options, configuration formats)
   - All dependencies and external integrations
   - All constraints you can identify or infer
   - All edge cases and error handling behavior
   - Design decisions and their apparent rationale

Think VERY hard about this. Be thorough. The quality of the final spec
depends entirely on how deeply you understand the source material now.
Miss something here and it will be missing from the spec, which means
it will be missing from the implementation.

## Parallel Research via Sub-Agents
The source material is a codebase of 19 files. Use sub-agents to research
distinct subsystems in parallel. Suggested groupings:

1. **Core engine & orchestration**: engine.ts, run-state.ts, run-status.ts, events.ts, timeout.ts
2. **Execution modes**: forward.ts, reverse.ts, decompose.ts, interactive.ts
3. **External integrations**: opencode.ts, beads.ts, skills.ts
4. **CLI & configuration**: index.ts, config.ts, init.ts, types.ts
5. **Output & progress**: output-parser.ts, progress.ts, template.ts

Launch multiple sub-agents concurrently, each researching a different
grouping. Give each sub-agent:
- Its specific scope (which files to examine)
- What to look for (features, behaviors, interfaces, constraints)
- The clean-room context: we are building a behavioral spec, not copying structure
- Instruction to return a structured research summary

When all sub-agents return, synthesize their findings into the analysis
document at `docs/specs/super-ralph-analysis.md`.

## Spawning Rules
After completing the analysis, if the source material is so large that
even with sub-agent research important areas weren't covered deeply
enough, spawn additional study beads:

  br create --parent bd-3hv \
    --title "Deep study: <area name>" \
    --type task \
    --description "<focused study instructions>"

  br dep add <new_bead_id> bd-3hv.1
  br dep add bd-3hv.2 <new_bead_id>

But prefer doing research in this bead via sub-agents — it's faster
and produces a more coherent analysis.

## Completion
br close bd-3hv.1 --reason "Analysis complete at docs/specs/super-ralph-analysis.md"

title: Fix spec: Console renderer and run tracker details
description:
## Problem
§5.2 (Event Emission Architecture) references two key listeners — the console renderer and the run tracker — but neither is fully specified.

### Gaps:

**Console Renderer:**
1. Not specified anywhere. §5.2 says "Console renderer (writes human-readable output to stdout)" but there is no section defining what it outputs for each of the 14 event types. What format? Colors? Icons? Indentation?
2. Does it write to stdout or stderr? (stdout conflicts with stream capture of sub-agent output)
3. What library is used for terminal formatting (if any)?

**Run Tracker:**
1. **Initialization**: When is the run tracker created? Before the iteration loop? During engine setup? What creates the runId?
2. **events.jsonl format**: Each line is "a complete JSON object with a type field" but the full schema isn't defined. Does it include a timestamp? The full payload from §5.1? Example entry?
3. **Incremental counter updates**: §5.4 says the run tracker incrementally updates counters from events, but which events increment which counters? The mapping is implied but not explicit.
4. **SessionState finalization**: The engine "finalizes the run" (§2.8) — what does this mean concretely? Setting `status` to `"completed"` or `"failed"`? Setting `updatedAt`?

## Clean-room test
An implementer would have no idea what the console output looks like. They'd also have to guess the events.jsonl format and when/how the run tracker is initialized. These are observable behaviors users interact with directly.

## What to add
- Add §5.5 Console Renderer: table of event type → console output format
- Add §5.6 Run Tracker: initialization, events.jsonl entry schema (with example), counter update mapping, finalization behavior
- Specify whether console output goes to stdout or stderr

title: Fix spec: task_complete plugin implementation contract
description:
## Problem
§3.6 describes the task_complete tool interface but lacks the plugin implementation details needed to actually build it.

### Gaps:
1. **Plugin API contract**: What does an OpenCode plugin JS file export? Is it a default export? A named export? A function? An object with tool definitions?
2. **Tool registration**: How does OpenCode discover and load the plugin? Does it scan .opencode/plugins/ automatically? Does package.json need a specific structure?
3. **Plugin behavior**: What does the plugin DO when task_complete is called? Just register the tool schema so the sub-agent can call it? Or does it need to handle the call and do something?
4. **package.json contents**: §3.1.7 says `.opencode/package.json` is created — what dependencies does it need? What's the structure?
5. **Tool state vs tool call**: §2.7 says scan for "a task_complete tool call where the tool state's status is 'completed'" — this implies two different "status" values: the tool's own state (e.g., "completed" meaning the tool call finished executing) and the input.status parameter (e.g., "complete" meaning the work is done). This distinction needs to be explicit.

## Clean-room test
An implementer would know they need a JS plugin file and what tool parameters it exposes, but they wouldn't be able to write the actual plugin file because the OpenCode plugin API contract is unspecified. They'd also likely confuse tool state "completed" with input status "complete".

## What to add
- Specify the plugin file structure (exports, tool definition format)
- Provide a complete example or pseudocode for the plugin
- Specify package.json contents
- Clarify the distinction between tool execution state ("completed") and task status input parameter ("complete", "phase_done", etc.)

title: Fix spec: Init command AGENTS.md and template source paths
description:
## Problem
§3.1.7 (init command) describes several artifacts but leaves key details unspecified.

### Gaps:
1. **AGENTS.md content**: The init creates .super-ralph/AGENTS.md but the spec never says what goes in it. What instructions does it contain? This is critical because the sub-agent reads it.

2. **Root AGENTS.md update**: "Root AGENTS.md MUST be updated with a reference to .super-ralph/AGENTS.md" — what reference? An include directive? A markdown link? Literal text? What if root AGENTS.md doesn't exist — create it?

3. **Template source location**: Templates (forward.hbs, decompose.hbs, etc.) are "copied during init" — copied from WHERE? A bundled resource? A path relative to the CLI installation? A URL?

4. **cli.path patching**: The config.toml has cli.path "patched to the current CLI installation path" — how is this path determined? `process.argv[0]`? `which super-ralph`? `import.meta.path`?

5. **intake-checklist.md content**: Created but never described. What does it contain? Is it a template or static content?

## Clean-room test
An implementer could create the directory structure but would produce empty or wrong AGENTS.md content, wouldn't know where templates come from, and would guess incorrectly on cli.path resolution. The AGENTS.md content is especially critical because it's what the sub-agent reads to understand its role.

## What to add
- Specify AGENTS.md content (or at minimum, its purpose and key sections)
- Specify how root AGENTS.md is updated (exact text/format)
- Specify template source paths (e.g., bundled in the CLI package at src/templates/)
- Specify cli.path resolution mechanism
- Specify intake-checklist.md content or purpose

title: Draft clean-room specification
description:
## Objective
Produce a clean-room specification from the analysis document. This is
the most important bead in the entire reverse process. Think deeply and
be extremely thorough.

## What "Clean-Room Specification" Means
You are writing a spec that someone could use to BUILD an equivalent
system from scratch, without ever seeing the original source material.

This means:
- Describe WHAT the system does, not HOW the source code is structured
- Every behavior must be specified precisely enough to implement
- Every interface must be defined completely (exact signatures, data
  formats, error types, status codes)
- Every constraint must be stated explicitly (performance targets,
  security requirements, compatibility needs)
- Every edge case must be documented with expected behavior
- Every design decision must include rationale

DO NOT simply reorganize or paraphrase the analysis. TRANSFORM it into
a specification. The analysis says "the system does X" — the spec must
say "the system MUST do X, because Y, and when Z happens it MUST
respond with W."

## The Analysis
Read ALL of `docs/specs/super-ralph-analysis.md`.

## Instructions
Create the clean-room specification at `docs/specs/super-ralph-spec.md`.

Structure the spec to cover:
1. Purpose and Scope
2. Behavior Specification (for every feature: observable behavior,
   triggers, inputs/outputs, state changes, errors, edge cases)
3. Interfaces (exact CLI commands, flags, options, environment variables, configuration file format)
4. Data Model (structures, fields, relationships, constraints)
5. Constraints (performance, security, compatibility, reliability)
6. Dependencies (external systems, versions, fallback behavior)
7. Edge Cases and Error Handling
8. Design Rationale (key decisions and WHY)

## Quality Standard
The spec MUST be detailed enough that:
- An engineer who has never seen the original source could build from it
- No section is vague or says "implementation-defined" without constraints
- Every "should" is actually a "must" — be prescriptive
- The spec could survive legal scrutiny as a clean-room document

Think VERY hard. This document is the foundation for everything that follows.

## Completion
br close bd-3hv.2 --reason "Clean-room spec created at docs/specs/super-ralph-spec.md"

title: Fresh eyes review pass 1
description:
## Objective
Carefully review this entire spec with completely fresh eyes. You have
NOT seen this spec before. You are reviewing it as a clean-room
specification — a document that must be complete enough to build from
without ever seeing the original source material.

## The Spec
Read ALL of `docs/specs/super-ralph-spec.md`.

## Review Instructions
Carefully review this entire spec and come up with your best revisions
in terms of better architecture, new features, changed features, etc.
to make it better, more robust/reliable, more performant, more
compelling/useful, etc.

For each issue found:
- Give your detailed analysis and rationale/justification for why the
  change would make the spec better
- If it's a minor fix (typo, formatting, small clarification), fix it
  directly in the spec
- If it's a significant gap or revision, create a fix-up bead

Apply the CLEAN-ROOM TEST to every section: "Could an engineer who has
never seen the original source implement this correctly from what's
written here?" If no — that's a gap.

Specifically look for:
- Behaviors described vaguely ("handles errors appropriately") instead
  of precisely ("returns HTTP 429 with Retry-After header")
- Interfaces missing parameter types, error types, or edge case behavior
- Constraints stated without numbers ("should be fast" vs "must respond
  within 50ms at p99")
- Design decisions stated without rationale (WHY is missing)
- Features mentioned in one section but not fully specified in another
- Missing sections for things obvious to someone who saw the source
  but invisible to someone reading only the spec
- Implicit dependencies or assumptions that aren't stated

DO NOT OVERSIMPLIFY THINGS! DO NOT LOSE ANY FEATURES OR FUNCTIONALITY!
The spec should be getting MORE detailed with each review pass, not less.

## Spawning Rules
For each SIGNIFICANT gap or needed revision:

  br create --parent bd-3hv \
    --title "Fix spec: <concise description>" \
    --type task \
    --description "<self-contained instructions: what the problem is,
      where in the spec it is, exactly what to add/change, and why.
      Include the clean-room test: what would an implementer get wrong
      or miss if this gap isn't filled?>"

  br dep add <new_bead_id> bd-3hv.3

## REQUIRED: Wire Dependencies After Spawning
For EACH fix-up bead you created, block the next review pass on it:

  br dep add bd-3hv.4 <fix_bead_id>

Verify: `br show bd-3hv.4` should list your fix-up beads.

## Stop Condition
If you find that issues from a previous pass are already being addressed
by other open beads, do not duplicate that work. Focus only on NEW issues.

## At the End
After integrating all revisions, report:
- Which changes you wholeheartedly agree improve the spec
- Which you somewhat agree with
- Which you considered but rejected, and why

## Completion
br close bd-3hv.3 --reason "Review pass 1: found N issues, created M fix-up beads"
close_reason: Review pass 1: found 9 issues, created 6 fix-up beads (bd-3hv.7 through bd-3hv.12). Fixed 3 minor issues directly in spec (run ID format precision, iteration delay placement in loop, retry invariant documentation).

description:

  br dep add <new_bead_id> bd-3hv.4

## REQUIRED: Wire Dependencies After Spawning
For EACH fix-up bead you created, block the next review pass on it:

  br dep add bd-3hv.5 <fix_bead_id>

Verify: `br show bd-3hv.5` should list your fix-up beads.

## Stop Condition
If you find that issues from a previous pass are already being addressed
by other open beads, do not duplicate that work. Focus only on NEW issues.

## Completion
br close bd-3hv.4 --reason "Review pass 2: found N issues, created M fix-up beads"

title: Fresh eyes review pass 3
description:
## Objective
Carefully review this entire spec with completely fresh eyes. You have
NOT seen this spec before. You are reviewing it as a clean-room
specification — a document that must be complete enough to build from
without ever seeing the original source material.

## The Spec
Read ALL of `docs/specs/super-ralph-spec.md`.

## Review Instructions
Carefully review this entire spec and come up with your best revisions
in terms of better architecture, new features, changed features, etc.
to make it better, more robust/reliable, more performant, more
compelling/useful, etc.

For each issue found:
- Give your detailed analysis and rationale/justification for why the
  change would make the spec better
- If it's a minor fix (typo, formatting, small clarification), fix it
  directly in the spec
- If it's a significant gap or revision, create a fix-up bead

Apply the CLEAN-ROOM TEST to every section: "Could an engineer who has
never seen the original source implement this correctly from what's
written here?" If no — that's a gap.

Specifically look for:
- Behaviors described vaguely ("handles errors appropriately") instead
  of precisely ("returns HTTP 429 with Retry-After header")
- Interfaces missing parameter types, error types, or edge case behavior
- Constraints stated without numbers ("should be fast" vs "must respond
  within 50ms at p99")
- Design decisions stated without rationale (WHY is missing)
- Features mentioned in one section but not fully specified in another
- Missing sections for things obvious to someone who saw the source
  but invisible to someone reading only the spec
- Implicit dependencies or assumptions that aren't stated

DO NOT OVERSIMPLIFY THINGS! DO NOT LOSE ANY FEATURES OR FUNCTIONALITY!
The spec should be getting MORE detailed with each review pass, not less.

## Spawning Rules
For each SIGNIFICANT gap or needed revision:

  br create --parent bd-3hv \
    --title "Fix spec: <concise description>" \
    --type task \
    --description "<self-contained instructions: what the problem is,
      where in the spec it is, exactly what to add/change, and why.
      Include the clean-room test: what would an implementer get wrong
      or miss if this gap isn't filled?>"

  br dep add <new_bead_id> bd-3hv.5

## REQUIRED: Wire Dependencies After Spawning
For EACH fix-up bead you created, block the consolidation bead on it
(since this is the last fixed review pass):

  br dep add bd-3hv.6 <fix_bead_id>

Verify: `br show bd-3hv.6` should list your fix-up beads.

## Adaptive Continuation
This is pass 3. If you found significant issues and created fix-up beads,
also create the next review pass:

  br create --parent bd-3hv \
    --title "Fresh eyes review pass 4" \
    --type task \
    --description "<same review instructions with updated pass number
      and adaptive continuation>"

  Wire the new pass to depend on your fix-up beads.
  Wire the consolidation bead (bd-3hv.6) to depend on it:
    br dep add bd-3hv.6 <new_pass_id>

If you found NO significant issues — the spec has reached steady state.
Close this bead normally. Do not spawn another pass.

## Stop Condition
If you find that issues from a previous pass are already being addressed
by other open beads, do not duplicate that work. Focus only on NEW issues.

## At the End
After integrating all revisions, report:
- Which changes you wholeheartedly agree improve the spec
- Which you somewhat agree with
- Which you considered but rejected, and why

## Completion
br close bd-3hv.5 --reason "Review pass 3: found N issues, created M fix-up beads"

title: Clean-room verification and consolidation
description:
## Objective
Final verification that this is a true clean-room specification, plus
formatting and consistency polish.

## The Clean-Room Test
Read ALL of `docs/specs/super-ralph-spec.md`.

For every section, ask: "Could an engineer who has NEVER seen the
original source material build this correctly from what's written here?"

Go section by section:
1. Are all behaviors precisely specified with exact expected outcomes?
2. Are all interfaces fully defined (signatures, types, errors, examples)?
3. Are all constraints stated with concrete numbers or criteria?
4. Are all edge cases documented with expected behavior?
5. Are all design decisions accompanied by rationale?
6. Is the spec free of references to "the source" or "the original" —
   it should be entirely self-standing?
7. Could this document survive legal scrutiny as clean-room work?

If ANY section fails the clean-room test, fix it now. This is the last
chance before the spec moves to decompose.

## Formatting and Consistency
1. Check heading levels are consistent throughout
2. Check list styles are uniform
3. Check code blocks have language tags
4. Check for contradictions between sections
5. Check that every "TBD", "TODO", or placeholder has been resolved
6. Check that section numbering is sequential and correct

## Final Assessment
At the end, write a brief assessment at the bottom of the spec:
- Confidence level that this spec is complete (high/medium/low)
- Any areas where the spec is weaker
- Recommendations for the decompose phase

## Completion
br close bd-3hv.6 --reason "Spec finalized — clean-room verified and ready for decompose"

title: Fix spec: OpenCode server startup and SDK integration protocol
description:
## Problem
§2.5 (Server Lifecycle), §2.1.3 (Iteration Loop steps 5d-5f), and §2.5.3 (SDK Client Setup) reference the OpenCode server and SDK but lack the concrete details an implementer needs.

### Gaps:
1. **Server startup command**: §2.5.1 says "start a new OpenCode server as a subprocess" but never specifies what command to run. Is it `opencode serve`? `opencode`? What arguments? What working directory?

2. **Server readiness detection**: After starting the server, how do we know it's ready? Is there a polling loop on `session.list()`? A fixed delay? A ready signal on stdout? What's the retry interval and max wait time?

3. **SDK client instantiation**: §2.5.3 says "v1 client" and "v2 client" but doesn't show how to construct them. What are the import paths? Constructor signatures? Is it `new OpenCodeClient(url)` or `createClient({url})`?

4. **Session creation**: §2.1.3 step 5d says "Create an OpenCode session with a descriptive name" but doesn't specify the API call or session name format.

5. **Prompt execution**: §2.1.3 step 5f says "Execute the prompt with dual timeout protection" but doesn't detail how prompts are sent. Is it `v2.promptAsync(sessionId, prompt, {model})`? What's the relationship between calling promptAsync and the SSE stream?

6. **SSE subscription**: §2.9.1 says "subscribe to the SSE event stream BEFORE sending the prompt" but doesn't specify the subscription API. Is it `v2.event.subscribe()` returning an AsyncIterator? What parameters (session ID filter)?

7. **Session abort**: §2.6.2 mentions "best-effort session abort" but no API is specified. Is it `v1.session.abort(sessionId)`?

8. **Session messages format**: §2.7 says "Fetch all session messages" — what does the response look like? What's the structure of a message object? How to find tool calls within messages?

## Clean-room test
An implementer would have to reverse-engineer the entire OpenCode SDK API surface from method names scattered through the spec. They would get server startup wrong, session creation wrong, and prompt execution wrong. This is the single biggest gap in the spec.

## What to add
Add a new section §2.5.4 (or expand §2.5) with:
- The exact `opencode` command and arguments for server startup
- Server readiness polling: interval (e.g., 500ms), max attempts, what endpoint to hit
- SDK client construction code (pseudocode or actual imports)
- Session creation API: `v1.session.create({name: "<format>"})` with name format
- Prompt execution API: `v2.promptAsync(sessionId, content, {model})` or equivalent
- SSE subscription API: `v2.event.subscribe()` and iterator interface
- Session abort API
- Session message list response format (enough to find task_complete tool calls)

title: Fix spec: Reverse phase output file lifecycle
description:
## Problem
§2.4.4 (Autonomous Mode) says "Read the current spec file from the output directory (if it exists)" but critical details are missing.

### Gaps:
1. **Output filename**: How is the spec filename determined? Is it derived from input materials? A fixed name like `spec.md`? Does the user provide it? The `--output` flag specifies a directory, not a file — so what's the file name within that directory?

2. **First iteration**: On the first iteration, no spec file exists yet. The prompt template receives the "current spec" — is it an empty string? null? Does the template have a conditional for this?

3. **Who writes the file**: The sub-agent presumably writes the spec file to disk. But this is never stated. Does the sub-agent write it? Does the engine write it after harvesting? The prompt must instruct the sub-agent to write to a specific path — what path?

4. **Filename for reverse prompt template**: §3.4.2 lists the reverse template context as "Phase-mode-dependent (inputs, current spec, skill content)" but doesn't enumerate the exact variables. What are the template variable names? `currentSpec`? `specPath`? `outputPath`? `inputs`?

## Clean-room test
An implementer would not know what filename to use for the output spec, how to handle the first iteration when no spec exists, or whether the engine or sub-agent writes the file. They'd have to guess.

## What to add
In §2.4.4, specify:
- How the output filename is determined (e.g., derived from first input's basename, or a default name)
- The full output file path construction: `<outputDir>/<filename>`
- First-iteration behavior: current spec is empty string or absent
- That the sub-agent writes the spec file to the output path (and the prompt tells it where)
- Enumerate exact template variables for the reverse prompt in §3.4.2

title: Fix spec: Interactive mode question tool protocol
description:
## Problem
§2.4.5 (Interactive Mode) references a "question" tool that the sub-agent calls, and §2.9.2 lists `question.asked` as an SSE event, but the full protocol is unspecified.

### Gaps:
1. **Question tool schema**: What parameters does the question tool accept? The spec mentions `select`, `multiselect`, and `text` types, and mentions "options" and "custom input allowed", but the actual parameter schema (JSON) is never defined.

2. **SSE event payload**: What fields does `question.asked` carry? It must include the question text, type, options list, etc. — but the payload isn't specified (unlike other events in §5.1 which have payload fields listed).

3. **Answer return mechanism**: §2.4.5 says "Return the user's answer to the sub-agent" but doesn't say HOW. Is there a REST API call? A tool response mechanism? What endpoint/method?

4. **Tool state detection**: §2.9.2 says `message.part.updated` shows "tool status changes" — is this how the question tool is detected, or is it the dedicated `question.asked` event? The relationship between these two event types for question handling is unclear.

5. **Multiselect with custom input fallback**: §2.4.5 says "If the user selects no options in a multiselect and custom input is allowed, fall back to text input" — what determines whether custom input is allowed? Is it a parameter in the question tool call?

## Clean-room test
An implementer would not be able to implement interactive mode. They'd know they need to render questions and return answers but wouldn't know the data format for questions, the API for returning answers, or how to detect questions in the SSE stream.

## What to add
- Define the question tool parameter schema (type, message, options[], allowCustom, etc.)
- Define the `question.asked` SSE event payload fields
- Specify the answer return mechanism (API call, endpoint, parameters)
- Clarify the relationship between `question.asked` events and `message.part.updated` events

title: All-in-One OpenCode UX
description: Make super-ralph runnable entirely from inside OpenCode. Add cli.path to config, update skills/commands to use bun run cli_path, make doctor interactive, add prereq validation to init. Design: docs/plans/2026-02-23-all-in-one-opencode-ux-design.md Plan: docs/plans/2026-02-23-all-in-one-opencode-ux-plan.md

title: Task 1: Add cli.path to types and config
description:
Add a [cli] section with a path field to the config system.

## Files to modify
- src/types.ts — Add `cli: { path: string }` to LoopConfig interface (after the `opencode` property)
- src/config.ts — Add `cli: { path: "" }` to DEFAULT_CONFIG, add cli merge in loadConfig return
- templates/super-ralph-config.toml — Append [cli] section with empty path and comment
- .super-ralph/config.toml — Append [cli] section with path = "/Users/mat/dev/farsight/agent-framework/src/index.ts"

## Acceptance Criteria
1. LoopConfig type has cli.path property
2. loadConfig() merges cli section from TOML
3. Both config files have [cli] section
4. `bun run typecheck` passes

## Quality Gates
- `bun run typecheck` must pass

title: Task 2: Make doctor print actionable fix commands
description:
Rewrite the cmdDoctor() function in src/index.ts to print specific fix commands for each failing check, and add new checks.

## Files to modify
- src/index.ts — Rewrite cmdDoctor()

## Changes
1. Add bun version check at the start (informational since we're running in bun)
   - On fail: Fix: curl -fsSL https://bun.sh/install | bash
2. For br CLI fail: Fix: curl -fsSL "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_rust/main/install.sh?$(date +%s)" | bash
3. For project not initialized fail: Fix: /superralph:init
4. For prompt template missing fail: Fix: /superralph:init
5. For config not found: Fix: /superralph:init
6. For OpenCode server not reachable: Fix: opencode serve --port 4096
7. For plugin missing: Fix: /superralph:init
8. Add new check: .beads/ workspace exists. On fail: Fix: br init
9. Add new check: cli.path is set in config AND the file exists on disk
   - If path set but file missing: Fix: /superralph:init to re-detect
   - If path not set: Fix: /superralph:init to set [cli] path

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Every failing check prints a "  Fix: <command>" line
2. New checks for bun, .beads/, and cli.path are present
3. `bun run typecheck` passes
4. `bun run src/index.ts doctor` runs successfully

## Quality Gates
- `bun run typecheck` must pass

title: Task 3: Update init skill
description:
Update the super-ralph-init skill to validate prerequisites, detect cli_path, and initialize the beads workspace.

## Files to modify
- skills/super-ralph-init/SKILL.md

## Changes
1. Add a new step after Step 1 (check existing .super-ralph) for prerequisite validation:
   - Check bun --version. If missing: offer install command curl -fsSL https://bun.sh/install | bash
   - Check br --version. If missing: offer install command curl -fsSL "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_rust/main/install.sh?$(date +%s)" | bash
   - Stop if user declines to install missing prereqs

2. Update the config creation step (Step 4) to detect and record cli_path:
   - Resolve ~/.agents/super-ralph/src/index.ts to absolute path
   - Write to [cli] path in config
   - Confirm path with user

3. Add a new step after directory creation to run br init if .beads/ doesn't exist

4. Update the completion report to show cli_path and beads workspace status

5. Update the checklist to include:
   - Validated bun is installed
   - Validated br is installed
   - cli_path detected and recorded
   - .beads/ workspace initialized

6. Update the idempotency table to include .beads/ and [cli] path entries

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Skill includes prerequisite validation with install commands
2. Config creation step detects and records cli_path
3. Beads workspace initialization step exists
4. Checklist and report are updated

## Quality Gates
- No TypeScript files modified, so no typecheck needed
- Manual review: read the skill and verify all sections are present

title: Task 4: Update PRD skill launch wizards
description:
Update the launch wizard sections in all 4 PRD skills that have them. Replace npx super-ralph with bun run <cli_path>.

## Files to modify
- skills/feature-prd/SKILL.md (launch wizard around lines 672-744)
- skills/bug-prd/SKILL.md (launch wizard around lines 377-399)
- skills/hotfix-prd/SKILL.md (launch wizard around lines 255-267)
- skills/refactor-prd/SKILL.md (launch wizard around lines 753-825)

Note: skills/plan-prd/SKILL.md has NO launch wizard — do not modify it.

## Changes (apply to ALL 4 files)
1. Add a preamble step before the launch options: "Read cli_path from .super-ralph/config.toml (the [cli] path field). If not set, error and tell the user to run /superralph:init."
2. Replace ALL occurrences of "npx super-ralph run" with "bun run <cli_path> run"
3. Replace ALL occurrences of "npx super-ralph status" with "bun run <cli_path> status"
4. Rename "Run headless" option to "Run now"
5. Change post-completion reminder from "npx super-ralph run --epic <ID>" to "/superralph:resume"

## Acceptance Criteria

2. All launch wizards include the cli_path config read preamble
3. plan-prd/SKILL.md is NOT modified

## Quality Gates
- grep -rn "npx super-ralph" skills/ should return 0 results (excluding _archived/)

title: Task 5: Update resume command
description:
Update the superralph:resume command to use bun run <cli_path> and fix the br list --parent bug.

## Files to modify
- commands/superralph:resume.md

## Changes
1. Add a step to read cli_path from .super-ralph/config.toml before offering options
2. Replace all "npx super-ralph run" with "bun run <cli_path> run"
3. Replace all "npx super-ralph status" with "bun run <cli_path> status"
4. Fix bug: the command references "br list --parent <epicId> --json" which doesn't exist.
   Change to: use "br show <epicId> --json" to get children from dependents array,
   and "br ready --parent <epicId> --json --limit 1" for the next ready bead.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. No "npx super-ralph" references remain
2. cli_path config read step is present before options
3. br commands are correct (no --parent on br list)

## Quality Gates
- grep -n "npx super-ralph" commands/superralph:resume.md returns 0 results
- grep -n "br list --parent" commands/superralph:resume.md returns 0 results

title: Task 6: Update README
description:
Update the README with prerequisites, corrected user flow, and in-agent execution description.

## Files to modify
- README.md

## Changes
1. Add a Prerequisites section after the intro listing:
   - OpenCode (https://opencode.ai) — AI coding agent
   - bun (https://bun.sh) — JavaScript runtime
   - br (https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_rust) — beads CLI for task tracking

2. Update Per-Project Setup to mention:
   - Prerequisite validation (bun, br)
   - cli_path auto-detection in config
   - .beads/ workspace initialization

3. Update Phase 2: Execution section:
   - Explain that execution happens from inside OpenCode via the launch wizard

4. Update the Updating section to note cli_path stays stable across git pull

## Acceptance Criteria

3. Phase 2 explains in-agent execution

## Quality Gates
- grep -n "npx super-ralph" README.md returns 0 results

title: Task 7: Verify and push
description:
Run typecheck, doctor, and dry-run to verify everything works. Then push all changes.

## Steps
1. Run `bun run typecheck` — must pass
2. Run `bun run src/index.ts doctor` — all checks should pass
3. Run `bun run src/index.ts run --epic bd-3fk --dry-run` — should show epic info without connecting to server
4. Run `git push`

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Typecheck passes with 0 errors
2. Doctor shows all green checks
3. Dry-run works without errors
4. Changes are pushed to origin

## Quality Gates
- bun run typecheck passes
- bun run src/index.ts doctor passes

title: Clean-Room Reverse Engineering Spec for Ralph TUI
description:
Create a clean-room, behavior-first specification for the ralph-tui repository in a spec/ folder.
Document architecture, workflows, module behavior, and coverage without implementation-level code commentary.

title: US-001: Establish spec framework and writing contract
description:
As the documentation consumer, I want a clear spec/ structure and documentation conventions so all reverse-engineered docs are consistent and easy to navigate.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Create spec/README.md defining purpose, scope, and navigation.
- [ ] Create spec/CONVENTIONS.md defining clean-room rules, terminology, and confidence labeling conventions.
- [ ] Define required section template for module docs (Purpose, Triggers, Inputs, Outputs, Side Effects, Failure Modes, Dependencies, Open Questions).
- [ ] spec/README.md includes a table linking all spec artifacts.
- [ ] `markdownlint "spec/**/*.md"` passes.
- [ ] `vale "spec/**/*.md"` passes.

title: US-002: Build complete module inventory
description:
As the documentation consumer, I want a full catalog of repository modules so coverage is explicit and trackable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Create spec/module-catalog.md listing all source modules grouped by domain.
- [ ] Each module entry includes path, responsibility summary, and status (covered, partial, todo).
- [ ] Every module appears exactly once in the catalog.
- [ ] Catalog links to its detailed module spec file when available.
- [ ] `markdownlint "spec/**/*.md"` passes.
- [ ] `vale "spec/**/*.md"` passes.

title: US-003: Document system architecture and runtime lifecycle
description:
As the documentation consumer, I want architecture and startup/runtime behavior documented so I can understand how the system operates end to end.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Create spec/architecture/overview.md describing major domains and boundaries.
- [ ] Create spec/architecture/runtime-lifecycle.md describing initialization, steady-state loop, and shutdown behavior.
- [ ] Include at least one text-based sequence/flow representation for primary runtime path.
- [ ] Identify entrypoints and cross-domain interaction boundaries.
- [ ] `markdownlint "spec/**/*.md"` passes.
- [ ] `vale "spec/**/*.md"` passes.

title: US-004: Document user-visible workflows and behavior contracts
description:
As the documentation consumer, I want key user workflows captured behaviorally so I can reason about expected outcomes and edge behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Create spec/workflows/core-workflows.md covering primary user actions and expected outcomes.
- [ ] For each workflow, document triggers, preconditions, normal flow, alternate flow, and failure outcomes.
- [ ] Include observable outputs/state changes for each workflow.
- [ ] Cross-reference participating modules for each workflow.
- [ ] `markdownlint "spec/**/*.md"` passes.
- [ ] `vale "spec/**/*.md"` passes.

title: US-005: Document module-level behavior specs by domain
description:
As the documentation consumer, I want deep module behavior documentation so I can understand each component in isolation and in context.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Create domain folders under spec/modules with one doc per module.
- [ ] Each module doc follows the required template from spec/CONVENTIONS.md.
- [ ] Module docs remain implementation-agnostic (no copied source, no line-by-line commentary).
- [ ] Each module doc includes explicit inbound and outbound dependencies.
- [ ] `markdownlint "spec/**/*.md"` passes.
- [ ] `vale "spec/**/*.md"` passes.

title: US-006: Document state, configuration, and persistence behavior
description:
As the documentation consumer, I want state and configuration behavior documented so I can understand data flow and persistence expectations.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Create spec/behavior/state-model.md describing key state entities and transitions.
- [ ] Create spec/behavior/configuration.md describing configuration sources, precedence, defaults, and validation behavior.
- [ ] Create spec/behavior/persistence-and-io.md describing read/write boundaries and external interactions.
- [ ] All documented behaviors link back to relevant module specs.
- [ ] `markdownlint "spec/**/*.md"` passes.
- [ ] `vale "spec/**/*.md"` passes.

title: US-007: Document errors, edge cases, and resilience behavior
description:
As the documentation consumer, I want failure behavior documented so operational and UX edge cases are understandable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Create spec/behavior/error-handling.md covering error classes and user-visible error outcomes.
- [ ] Document retry/recovery/abort behavior where applicable.
- [ ] Document at least one negative-path scenario per core workflow.
- [ ] Mark unknown or inferred behaviors explicitly as open questions or assumptions.
- [ ] `markdownlint "spec/**/*.md"` passes.
- [ ] `vale "spec/**/*.md"` passes.

title: US-008: Produce coverage matrix and final gap report
description:
As the documentation consumer, I want proof of completeness so I can trust the spec as a working reference.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Create spec/coverage-matrix.md mapping every cataloged module to at least one detailed spec section.
- [ ] Create spec/gap-report.md listing unresolved ambiguities and non-critical gaps.
- [ ] Coverage matrix shows 100% module coverage against spec/module-catalog.md.
- [ ] spec/README.md is updated with final artifact index and completion status.
- [ ] Human coverage audit confirms all modules/behaviors are documented with no unresolved critical gaps.
- [ ] `markdownlint "spec/**/*.md"` passes.
- [ ] `vale "spec/**/*.md"` passes.

title: E2E Test: SDK Loop Validation
description: Test epic to validate the super-ralph OpenCode SDK execution loop end-to-end. Contains small, self-contained tasks that exercise the full pipeline: prompt rendering, agent execution, task_complete tool calls, bead closure, and progress tracking.

title: US-001: Add quality gate commands to .super-ralph/AGENTS.md
description:
## Description

The quality gates section in .super-ralph/AGENTS.md currently has placeholder commands (commented out). Since this project now has a TypeScript codebase with `bun run typecheck` as the primary quality gate, update the quality gates section to include the actual command.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Replace the commented-out placeholder commands in the Quality Gates section of `.super-ralph/AGENTS.md` with the actual project commands:
   - Typecheck: `bun run typecheck`
2. Keep the existing structure and explanatory text
3. Apply the same change to `templates/agents.md` (the template copy) so future projects get the correct defaults

## Quality Gates
- `bun run typecheck` must pass

title: US-002: Add .gitignore entries for beads database
description:
## Description

The .beads/ directory contains a SQLite database (beads.db) that should not be tracked by git — only the JSONL file matters. Add appropriate .gitignore entries.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Verify the existing .beads/.gitignore handles the database file correctly
2. If not, update it to ignore beads.db and any WAL/SHM files (beads.db-wal, beads.db-shm)
3. The issues.jsonl, config.yaml, and metadata.json files MUST remain tracked
4. Verify with `git status` that no database files show as untracked

## Quality Gates
- `bun run typecheck` must pass
- `git status` shows clean state after changes

title: REVIEW-001: Verify SDK loop migration completeness
description:
## Description

Review the entire super-ralph codebase for any remaining stale references from the SDK loop migration. This is a fresh-eyes review — check everything systematically.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Search ALL files (not just skills/) for any remaining references to:
   - `ralph-tui` (the old execution tool) — should only appear in archived skills, old design docs, and git history
   - `bd ` (the old Go beads CLI) — should only appear in archived skills
   - `.ralph-tui/` (the old config directory) — should not appear in any active file
   - `<promise>COMPLETE</promise>` (the old completion signal) — should be completely gone
2. Document any findings in .super-ralph/progress.md
3. If stale references are found in active files, fix them
4. Run `bun run typecheck` to ensure no TypeScript issues

## Quality Gates
- `bun run typecheck` must pass
- `grep -rn 'ralph-tui' commands/ skills/ src/ templates/ .super-ralph/ .opencode/ README.md` returns 0 results (excluding _archived/)

title: Smoke Test: SDK Loop
Add a comment to src/types.ts
description: Add a single-line comment at the top of src/types.ts that says '// Smoke test: SDK loop validated'. Do not change anything else. When done, call the task_complete tool with status 'complete'.
